properties.name: Epoch_Key_Access_Control
properties.claim: Credentials required to generate operational group keys SHALL only be accessible to Nodes with privilege level deemed as group members.
properties.violation: Unprivileged nodes gain access to epoch keys, enabling them to derive operational group keys and decrypt all group communications. This breaks group isolation and allows unauthorized nodes to impersonate group members, compromising confidentiality and authentication guarantees.
properties.proverifQuery: query x:principal, e:key ⊢ event access_epoch_key(x, e) ==> event is_privileged_member(x)

properties.name: Shared_Key_Computational_Infeasibility
properties.claim: Access to shared keys SHALL be computationally infeasible for non-trusted parties, barring software error or compromise of privileged Node.
properties.violation: Non-trusted parties derive operational group keys through cryptographic weaknesses or implementation flaws. Attackers decrypt group messages, forge authenticated communications, and gain unauthorized access to encrypted data, completely breaking the security model.
properties.proverifQuery: query a:principal, k:key ⊢ event derive_key(a, k) ==> event is_trusted(a) | event compromised_node_exists()

properties.name: Group_ID_Uniqueness
properties.claim: Administrators SHALL assign Group IDs such that no two operational groups within a Fabric have the same Group ID.
properties.formal: ∀g1, g2, fabric. (g1 ≠ g2 ∧ in_fabric(g1, fabric) ∧ in_fabric(g2, fabric)) ⟹ group_id(g1) ≠ group_id(g2)
properties.violation: Two distinct operational groups share the same Group ID, causing nodes to incorrectly route messages, derive keys for wrong security domains, and decrypt messages intended for other groups. This breaks group isolation and causes authentication failures or unauthorized data access across security boundaries.
properties.proverifQuery: query g1:group, g2:group, f:fabric ⊢ event same_group_id(g1, g2, f) ==> g1 = g2

properties.name: Epoch_Key_Randomness
properties.claim: Each epoch key SHALL be a random value of length CRYPTO_SYMMETRIC_KEY_LENGTH_BITS bits generated by Crypto_DRBG.
properties.violation: Epoch keys are predictable or reused, allowing attackers to precompute or guess keys. This enables unauthorized derivation of operational group keys, breaking all group security guarantees including confidentiality, authentication, and membership proof.
properties.proverifQuery: query e:key ⊢ event generated_epoch_key(e) ==> event is_random(e) & event correct_length(e)

properties.name: Current_Epoch_Key_For_Sending
properties.claim: Nodes sending group messages SHALL use operational group keys derived from the current epoch key (latest start time that is not in the future).
properties.formal: ∀node, msg, key, time. send(node, msg, key, time) ⟹ key = derive(current_epoch_key(time)) ∧ start_time(current_epoch_key(time)) ≤ time
properties.violation: Sending nodes use operational keys derived from future or outdated epoch keys. Receivers cannot decrypt messages if they lack the corresponding epoch key, causing communication failures. If old keys are used after intended expiration, revoked nodes retain access to group communications.
properties.proverifQuery: query n:principal, m:bitstring, k:key, t:time ⊢ event send_message(n, m, k, t) ==> event is_current_key(k, t)

properties.name: Accept_All_Installed_Epoch_Keys
properties.claim: Nodes receiving group messages SHALL accept the use of any key derived from currently installed epoch keys, regardless of whether start time is future or past.
properties.formal: ∀node, msg, key. receive(node, msg, key) ∧ has_epoch_key_for(node, key) ⟹ accept(node, msg)
properties.violation: Receiving nodes reject messages encrypted with valid epoch keys due to timing constraints. This causes legitimate group communications to fail during key rotation windows, breaking availability and creating operational gaps where valid members cannot communicate.
properties.proverifQuery: query n:principal, m:bitstring, k:key ⊢ event receive_message(n, m, k) & event has_epoch_key(n, k) ==> event accept_message(n, m)

properties.name: Epoch_Key_Rotation_Bounds
properties.claim: For every group key set, there SHALL be at least 1 and at most 3 epoch keys in rotation.
properties.violation: Key sets violate cardinality bounds. Zero keys cause complete group communication failure. More than 3 keys increase attack surface (more keys to protect) and complicate rotation logic, potentially creating synchronization vulnerabilities where nodes have inconsistent key states.
properties.proverifQuery: query ks:keyset ⊢ event key_set_size(ks, n) ==> (n >= 1) & (n <= 3)

properties.name: EpochKey0_Mandatory_In_Updates
properties.claim: Any epoch key update SHALL include EpochKey0 and MAY include EpochKey1 and EpochKey2.
properties.violation: Key updates omit EpochKey0, causing nodes to lose the base key required for deterministic key ordering. Nodes cannot determine current epoch key, leading to message encryption/decryption failures and loss of group connectivity. The ordered key rotation state machine breaks.
properties.proverifQuery: query u:update ⊢ event key_update(u) ==> event contains_epoch_key0(u)

properties.name: Atomic_Key_Set_Replacement
properties.claim: Any update of the key set, including partial update, SHALL remove all previous keys in the set and replace them atomically.
properties.formal: ∀keySet, update. apply_update(keySet, update) ⟹ (∀k ∈ old_keys(keySet). k ∉ new_keys(keySet)) ∧ atomic(update)
properties.violation: Partial updates leave old keys installed alongside new keys, violating the intended key rotation. This allows revoked members who possess old keys to continue accessing group communications indefinitely. Race conditions during updates may create states where some nodes have mixed old/new keys, breaking message delivery.
properties.proverifQuery: query ks:keyset, u:update ⊢ event apply_update(ks, u) ==> event all_old_keys_removed(ks) & event atomic_operation(u)

properties.name: IPK_Key_Set_Reservation
properties.claim: The Group Key Set ID of 0 SHALL be reserved for managing the Identity Protection Key (IPK) on a given Fabric.
properties.violation: Non-IPK key sets use ID 0, causing collision with IPK management. This breaks identity protection mechanisms, potentially exposing Node identities or causing IPK operations to affect wrong key sets. Identity tracking becomes possible, violating privacy guarantees.
properties.proverifQuery: query ks:keyset ⊢ event key_set_id_zero(ks) ==> event is_ipk(ks)

properties.name: IPK_Irremovability
properties.claim: It SHALL NOT be possible to remove the IPK Key Set if it exists on a Fabric.
properties.violation: IPK key set is removed, breaking identity protection for all nodes in the fabric. Node identities become permanently exposed in all communications. This is unrecoverable without re-commissioning the entire fabric, and violates privacy guarantees fundamental to the security model.
properties.proverifQuery: query f:fabric ⊢ event remove_key_set(IPK(f)) ==> false

properties.name: Group_Session_Type_Requires_Group_Session_ID
properties.claim: When Session Type is 1 (group session), the Session ID SHALL be a Group Session ID as derived from the operational group key.
properties.violation: Group messages use incorrect Session IDs, causing receivers to search for keys using wrong identifiers. Messages fail to decrypt even when nodes possess correct keys. This breaks group communication and creates denial-of-service vulnerability where attacker sends malformed session IDs to exhaust receiver resources.
properties.proverifQuery: query m:bitstring ⊢ event session_type_group(m) ==> event has_valid_group_session_id(m)

properties.name: All_Key_Candidates_Attempted
properties.claim: On receipt of group message, all valid installed operational group key candidates referenced by Group Session ID SHALL be attempted until authentication passes or no keys remain.
properties.formal: ∀node, msg, gid. receive(node, msg, gid) ⟹ ∀k ∈ candidates(node, gid). try_decrypt(msg, k) until success ∨ exhausted(candidates)
properties.violation: Receivers stop trying keys prematurely, failing to decrypt valid messages when Group Session ID collision occurs. Legitimate communications are rejected despite correct keys being installed. This creates availability vulnerability and denies service to valid group members.
properties.proverifQuery: query n:principal, m:bitstring, gid:sessionid ⊢ event receive_group_message(n, m, gid) ==> event tried_all_candidates(n, gid) | event successful_decrypt(n, m)

properties.name: Group_Security_Info_Constant
properties.claim: The group security info SHALL be the fixed byte stream 'GroupKey v1.0' for all operational key derivations in this version.
properties.violation: Implementations use wrong security info strings, causing nodes to derive different operational keys from the same epoch keys. Group members cannot communicate despite possessing correct epoch keys. The domain separation intended by versioned info strings breaks, potentially allowing cross-version attacks.
properties.proverifQuery: query d:derivation ⊢ event derive_operational_key(d) ==> event correct_info_string(d)

properties.name: Per_Fabric_Key_Material
properties.claim: With exception of group security info, all input key material SHALL be maintained on a per-Fabric basis with no cross-Fabric sharing.
properties.formal: ∀f1, f2, key. (f1 ≠ f2 ∧ epoch_key(key, f1)) ⟹ ¬epoch_key(key, f2)
properties.violation: Key material is shared across Fabrics, allowing nodes in one Fabric to decrypt communications in another Fabric. This breaks Fabric isolation, a fundamental security boundary. Compromise of one Fabric immediately compromises all Fabrics sharing keys. Multi-tenancy security model collapses.
properties.proverifQuery: query f1:fabric, f2:fabric, k:key ⊢ event epoch_key_in_fabric(k, f1) & event epoch_key_in_fabric(k, f2) ==> f1 = f2

properties.name: KDF_Determinism
properties.claim: Operational group key derivation via Crypto_KDF SHALL produce identical output given identical epoch key and CompressedFabricIdentifier inputs.
properties.violation: Non-deterministic KDF produces different operational keys on each derivation despite same inputs. Nodes derive different keys from identical epoch keys, making group communication impossible. Every message fails to decrypt, causing complete group failure. This breaks fundamental cryptographic assumptions.
properties.proverifQuery: query e:key, f:fabric ⊢ event derive(e, f, k1) & event derive(e, f, k2) ==> k1 = k2

properties.name: Group_Session_ID_Derivation_Determinism
properties.claim: Group Session ID derived from operational group key via Crypto_KDF SHALL be deterministic and computed as big-endian 16-bit value.
properties.violation: Non-deterministic or incorrectly computed Group Session IDs prevent receivers from efficiently locating decryption keys. Every message requires exhaustive search through all installed keys, causing performance degradation. Incorrect endianness causes identifier mismatches, breaking group communication.
properties.proverifQuery: query ogk:key ⊢ event derive_group_session_id(ogk, gid1) & event derive_group_session_id(ogk, gid2) ==> gid1 = gid2

properties.name: Membership_Via_Epoch_Key_Possession
properties.claim: Group membership is determined exclusively by Node's possession of all epoch keys required to generate current valid operational group key.
properties.formal: ∀node, group. is_member(node, group) ⟺ possesses_epoch_keys(node, group) ∧ can_derive_current_key(node, group)
properties.violation: Membership enforcement fails, allowing nodes without proper epoch keys to be treated as members or vice versa. Unauthorized nodes send messages accepted as authentic. Legitimate members are rejected. This breaks the trust model where only key possession should determine access.
properties.proverifQuery: query n:principal, g:group ⊢ event is_member(n, g) <==> event has_epoch_keys(n, g)

properties.name: Key_Update_Idempotency
properties.claim: Key updates are idempotent operations ensuring Administrator is always source of truth; repeated identical updates produce same final state.
properties.formal: ∀ks, u. apply_update(apply_update(ks, u), u) = apply_update(ks, u)
properties.violation: Non-idempotent updates cause state divergence when retries occur during network failures. Nodes may apply same update multiple times with different outcomes, creating inconsistent key states across the group. Some nodes cannot decrypt messages from others despite successful key distribution.
properties.proverifQuery: query ks:keyset, u:update ⊢ event apply_twice(ks, u, result1) & event apply_once(ks, u, result2) ==> result1 = result2

properties.name: Epoch_Key_Ordering_Oldest_To_Newest
properties.claim: Epoch key update SHALL order the keys from oldest to newest based on start times.
properties.formal: ∀update. contains(update, [k0, k1, k2]) ⟹ start_time(k0) ≤ start_time(k1) ≤ start_time(k2)
properties.violation: Misordered keys cause nodes to use wrong epoch key as 'current'. Senders encrypt with key that receivers consider old or future. Messages fail to decrypt during rotation periods. Time-sync-less nodes using relative ordering select wrong keys, breaking their group connectivity.
properties.proverifQuery: query u:update ⊢ event key_update(u, k0, k1, k2) ==> event ordered(start_time(k0), start_time(k1), start_time(k2))

properties.name: Revocation_Via_Key_Withdrawal
properties.claim: Denying access to updated epoch keys serves as means to eject group members; nodes not receiving new keys lose access after key rotation.
properties.violation: Nodes retain access to group after supposed revocation by continuing to use old epoch keys. Administrators cannot remove compromised or unauthorized members. Revocation mechanism fails, allowing expelled nodes indefinite group access until keys are explicitly deleted (which may never happen in misconfigured systems).
properties.proverifQuery: query n:principal, g:group, t:time ⊢ event not_updated(n, g, t) & event key_rotated(g, t) ==> eventually(event cannot_communicate(n, g))

properties.name: Key_Activation_At_Start_Time
properties.claim: Epoch key becomes active for use by transmitting Nodes at the absolute UTC start time; keys SHALL NOT be used for sending before start time.
properties.violation: Senders use epoch keys before activation time, encrypting messages with keys that receivers have not yet activated. During synchronized rollout, messages sent early are rejected even though all nodes possess the key. Creates communication blackout windows during planned key rotations.
properties.proverifQuery: query k:key, t:time ⊢ event send_message_with_key(k, t) ==> t >= event start_time(k)

properties.name: Key_Propagation_Complete_Before_Activation
properties.claim: Start time for new epoch key is scheduled after key propagation interval to ensure Administrator synchronizes all Nodes with new key before activation.
properties.violation: New epoch key activated before all nodes receive it. Senders using new key encrypt messages that receivers cannot decrypt. Group communication partially fails during rotation, with some node pairs unable to communicate. Network partitioning or slow nodes cause permanent communication failures.
properties.proverifQuery: query k:key, g:group ⊢ event activate_key(k, g, t_activate) ==> event all_nodes_received(k, g, t_distribute) & (t_activate >= t_distribute + propagation_interval)

properties.name: Continued_Acceptance_Until_Explicit_Deletion
properties.claim: Nodes continue to accept communication secured under epoch key until that key is withdrawn by explicitly deleting it from Node's group state.
properties.violation: Nodes automatically expire epoch keys based on implicit timeout, rejecting valid messages from nodes still using those keys. During distributed key rotation, timing skew causes some nodes to expire keys while others still use them. Group communication becomes unreliable with random message failures.
properties.proverifQuery: query n:principal, k:key ⊢ event has_key(n, k) & event not_deleted(k) ==> event accepts_key(n, k)

properties.name: No_Cross_Group_Key_Sharing_For_Unrelated_Functions
properties.claim: Operational groups which do not share related functionality (e.g., lighting and sprinkler) SHOULD NOT share the same operational key.
properties.violation: Unrelated groups share keys, violating least-privilege. Compromise of one group (e.g., low-security lighting) immediately compromises unrelated groups (e.g., high-security door locks). Blast radius of key compromise is unnecessarily large. Attacker gains access to unrelated systems via single key theft.
properties.proverifQuery: query g1:group, g2:group ⊢ event same_key(g1, g2) & event unrelated(g1, g2) ==> false

properties.name: Group_Session_ID_Not_Sole_Key_Locator
properties.claim: Group Session ID SHALL NOT be used as sole means to locate operational group key due to potential collisions; all candidates must be tried.
properties.formal: ∀node, msg, gid. locate_key(node, msg, gid) ⟹ tries_all_candidates(node, gid) ∧ ¬relies_solely_on(gid)
properties.violation: Implementation uses Group Session ID as unique identifier without trying all candidates. When collision occurs (probability 2^-16), receiver uses wrong key and rejects valid message without trying other candidates. This creates exploitable denial-of-service where attacker induces collisions to block legitimate communications.
properties.proverifQuery: query n:principal, m:bitstring, gid:sessionid ⊢ event locate_key_solely_by_gid(n, m, gid) ==> false

properties.name: MIC_Authentication_Required
properties.claim: Message authentication SHALL verify via MIC; successful MIC verification confirms correct operational key and group membership.
properties.formal: ∀msg, key. accept(msg, key) ⟹ verify_mic(msg, key) = true
properties.violation: Messages accepted without MIC verification allow attacker to send unauthenticated messages to group. Forged commands are processed as legitimate. Even without key knowledge, attacker manipulates group behavior by replaying or crafting messages. Authentication guarantee completely breaks.
properties.proverifQuery: query m:bitstring, k:key ⊢ event accept_message(m, k) ==> event verify_mic(m, k)

properties.name: Group_Session_ID_Collision_Probability
properties.claim: Group Session ID collision probability is 2^-16, but collision of both Group Session ID and message MIC is 2^-80, providing sufficient security.
properties.violation: If KDF for Group Session ID is weakened or MIC is shortened, collision probability increases dramatically. Attacker can craft messages that collide on both Group Session ID and MIC, impersonating group members. The 2^-80 security margin is compromised, making brute-force attacks feasible.
properties.proverifQuery: query m1:bitstring, m2:bitstring, k1:key, k2:key ⊢ event collision(group_session_id(k1), group_session_id(k2)) & event collision(mic(m1,k1), mic(m2,k2)) & (k1 <> k2) ==> event probability_below_threshold(2^-80)

properties.name: Administrator_Sole_Key_Generator
properties.claim: Epoch keys are generated, managed, and stored exclusively by Administrator; nodes receive but never generate epoch keys.
properties.violation: Regular nodes generate their own epoch keys, breaking centralized trust model. Administrator loses control over group membership. Unauthorized nodes self-admit to groups by generating keys and sharing them. Key distribution policy cannot be enforced. The security boundary between administrator and member collapses.
properties.proverifQuery: query k:key ⊢ event epoch_key_generated(k, source) ==> event is_administrator(source)

properties.name: Unsynchronized_Nodes_Use_Relative_Timing
properties.claim: Nodes without time synchronization calculate current epoch key by comparing relative start times, using key with second-newest time until next Administrator contact.
properties.violation: Unsynchronized nodes incorrectly select current key, using newest (future) key instead of second-newest. They encrypt messages with keys other nodes have not activated, causing decrypt failures. Group communication fails for all non-time-synced devices (e.g., sleepy end devices, battery-powered sensors).
properties.proverifQuery: query n:principal ⊢ event no_time_sync(n) & event selects_key(n, k) ==> event is_second_newest(k, n)

properties.name: ICD_Update_Before_Key_Activation
properties.claim: Administrator SHOULD update Intermittently Connected Devices (ICDs) without time sync before new epoch key is activated, then let time-synced nodes roll to new key at synchronized time.
properties.violation: ICDs not updated before key activation continue using old keys after network has switched to new keys. ICD messages cannot be decrypted by other nodes. ICDs cannot decrypt received messages. Low-power devices lose group connectivity and remain partitioned until their next scheduled Administrator contact, possibly days or weeks.
properties.proverifQuery: query icd:principal, k:key ⊢ event no_time_sync(icd) & event key_activated(k, t_activate) ==> event updated(icd, k, t_update) & (t_update < t_activate)

properties.name: Key_Rotation_State_Machine_Correctness
properties.claim: Epoch key rotation follows defined state machine: Admin Refresh → Epoch Activate → Admin Update → Epoch Activate, maintaining key slot semantics (New/Current/Previous/Old).
properties.formal: state ∈ {AdminRefresh, EpochActivate, AdminUpdate} ∧ transitions = {AdminRefresh → EpochActivate → AdminUpdate → EpochActivate}
properties.violation: State machine transitions occur out of order or skip states. Keys in wrong semantic slots (Current used as Previous). Nodes have inconsistent understanding of which key is current. Some nodes use key A as current while others use key B, causing mutual decrypt failure and group partition.
properties.proverifQuery: query s1:state, s2:state ⊢ event transition(s1, s2) ==> event valid_transition(s1, s2)

properties.name: Maximum_Start_Time_Disables_Key
properties.claim: Epoch key marked with maximum start time (0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF) SHALL be disabled and render corresponding epoch key slot unused.
properties.violation: Keys with maximum start time treated as active with far-future activation. Nodes wait indefinitely for these keys to activate or incorrectly use them as current. Key slot accounting breaks, potentially allowing more than 3 keys in rotation. State machine cannot distinguish disabled slots from future keys.
properties.proverifQuery: query k:key ⊢ event start_time_max(k) ==> event disabled(k)

properties.name: ACL_Restricts_Key_Material_To_Admin
properties.claim: Key material exposed via Group Key Management Cluster attributes with ACL entries SHALL allow access only by key distribution Administrator.
properties.violation: ACL misconfiguration allows non-administrator nodes to read epoch keys from Group Key Management Cluster. Any node in fabric can retrieve all group keys, join any group without authorization, and decrypt all group communications. Entire group security model collapses to network-level access control.
properties.proverifQuery: query n:principal, km:attribute ⊢ event access_key_material(n, km) ==> event is_admin(n)

properties.name: Administrator_Complete_Group_Knowledge
properties.claim: Administrator SHALL possess all epoch keys, knowledge of all group security domain members, and responsibility for pushing updates to all authorized nodes.
properties.formal: ∀admin, group. manages(admin, group) ⟹ has_all_keys(admin, group) ∧ knows_all_members(admin, group) ∧ responsible_for_updates(admin, group)
properties.violation: Administrator lacks complete view of group membership or key state. Some nodes never receive key updates, becoming orphaned during rotation. Administrator cannot effectively revoke members it doesn't know about. Unauthorized nodes may possess keys without administrator knowledge, creating undetectable group penetration.
properties.proverifQuery: query a:principal, g:group ⊢ event manages_group(a, g) ==> event has_complete_knowledge(a, g)

properties.name: Commissioned_Node_Group_Installation_Sequence
properties.claim: Installing group on newly commissioned node follows sequence: (1) KeySetWrite with key set, (2) Write GroupKeyMap associating group ID with key set, (3) AddGroup to associate endpoint with group ID.
properties.formal: ∀node, group. install_group(node, group) ⟹ happens_before(KeySetWrite, GroupKeyMap_write) ∧ happens_before(GroupKeyMap_write, AddGroup)
properties.violation: Out-of-order operations cause incomplete group installation. AddGroup before KeySetWrite leaves node trying to use non-existent keys. GroupKeyMap without KeySetWrite creates dangling reference. Node appears to be in group but cannot send/receive messages. State inconsistency requires manual remediation.
properties.proverifQuery: query n:principal, g:group ⊢ event install_group(n, g) ==> event ordered_sequence(KeySetWrite, GroupKeyMap, AddGroup)

properties.name: IPv6_Multicast_Subscription_On_Group_Join
properties.claim: When group is associated with endpoint via AddGroup, node subscribes to IPv6 multicast address generated from fabric ID and group ID.
properties.formal: ∀node, group, fabric. AddGroup(node, group, fabric) ⟹ subscribes(node, ipv6_multicast(fabric, group))
properties.violation: Node fails to subscribe to multicast address despite successful AddGroup. It possesses correct keys and group membership but never receives groupcast messages at network layer. Node can send to group but appears deaf to incoming messages, creating asymmetric communication failure.
properties.proverifQuery: query n:principal, g:group, f:fabric ⊢ event add_group(n, g, f) ==> event subscribed_multicast(n, ipv6_multicast(f, g))

properties.name: Group_Key_Lifetime_Bounded_By_Epoch
properties.claim: Operational group key lifetime is limited by assigning expiration time to epoch key; constraining epoch key validity to epoch constrains operational group key to specific time periods.
properties.violation: Operational keys used beyond epoch key validity period due to lack of enforced expiration. Revoked members continue using old operational keys indefinitely. Time-bounded access control fails. Temporary group membership (e.g., guest access) cannot be enforced cryptographically.
properties.proverifQuery: query ok:key, ek:key, t:time ⊢ event derived_from(ok, ek) & event use_key(ok, t) ==> event valid_at_time(ek, t)

properties.name: Multi_Group_Membership_Support
properties.claim: Individual nodes can be members of multiple operational groups simultaneously; set of groups to which node belongs can change over time.
properties.violation: Node state management fails to track multiple group memberships correctly. Key material from different groups interferes or overwrites each other. Node can only be in one group at a time, limiting application flexibility. Cross-group operations (e.g., bridge devices) become impossible.
properties.proverifQuery: query n:principal, g1:group, g2:group ⊢ event member_of(n, g1) & event member_of(n, g2) ==> g1 <> g2 & event supports_multi_group(n)

properties.name: Subgroups_Share_Security_Domain
properties.claim: Multiple operational groups MAY share the same operational group key to create logical subgroups over shared security domain, differentiated by distinct Group IDs.
properties.formal: ∀g1, g2. operational_key(g1) = operational_key(g2) ∧ group_id(g1) ≠ group_id(g2) ⟹ shared_security_domain(g1, g2)
properties.violation: Implementation enforces one-to-one mapping between group IDs and keys, preventing subgroup creation. Applications cannot organize logical subgroups (e.g., room-based lighting zones sharing facility-wide security). This forces either excessive key management (one key per subgroup) or loss of subgroup isolation.
properties.proverifQuery: query g1:group, g2:group ⊢ event same_key(g1, g2) & event different_id(g1, g2) ==> event valid_subgroup_config(g1, g2)

properties.name: Administrator_Unique_Group_ID_Allocation
properties.claim: Administrator has sufficient access to centralized knowledge to allocate unique Group IDs under given Fabric ensuring no collisions.
properties.formal: ∀admin, fabric. manages(admin, fabric) ⟹ has_centralized_knowledge(admin, fabric) ∧ can_ensure_uniqueness(admin)
properties.violation: Multiple administrators operate without coordination, allocating overlapping Group IDs. Two groups receive same ID from different administrators. Nodes receive conflicting group configurations. Messages routed to wrong groups. The assumption of centralized knowledge is violated in practice.
properties.proverifQuery: query a:principal, f:fabric ⊢ event allocate_group_id(a, f, gid) ==> event unique_in_fabric(gid, f)

properties.name: Key_Set_Removal_Completeness
properties.claim: Administrator MAY completely remove group key set from node using KeySetRemove command, deleting all epoch keys and rendering group inaccessible.
properties.formal: ∀node, keyset. KeySetRemove(node, keyset) ⟹ ∀k ∈ keyset. deleted(k) ∧ ¬can_access_group(node, group(keyset))
properties.violation: KeySetRemove leaves residual key material or group state. Node retains partial group access or stale keys. Subsequent key additions may fail due to state corruption. Security-critical group removal (e.g., after node compromise) is incomplete, allowing continued unauthorized access.
properties.proverifQuery: query n:principal, ks:keyset ⊢ event remove_key_set(n, ks) ==> event all_keys_deleted(n, ks) & event no_group_access(n, group(ks))

properties.name: Operational_Key_Security_Properties
properties.claim: Operational group keys enable nodes to: prove group membership, exchange messages confidentially without fear of manipulation by non-members, and encrypt data decryptable only by group members.
properties.formal: ∀n1, n2, msg, key. in_group(n1,g) ∧ in_group(n2,g) ∧ operational_key(key,g) ⟹ can_prove_membership(n1,n2,key) ∧ confidential(msg,key,g) ∧ authentic(msg,key,g)
properties.violation: One or more security properties fail: nodes cannot prove membership (impersonation possible), messages lack confidentiality (eavesdropping possible), or messages lack integrity (manipulation undetected). This represents catastrophic failure of fundamental security guarantees promised by the group key mechanism.
properties.proverifQuery: query n1:principal, n2:principal, m:bitstring, k:key, g:group ⊢ event in_group(n1,g) & event in_group(n2,g) & event send(n1,n2,m,k,g) ==> event proves_membership(n1,n2,k) & event confidential(m,k) & event authentic(m,k)

assumptions.statement: Barring software error or compromise of a privileged Node, access to shared keys is computationally infeasible for non-trusted parties.
assumptions.impact_if_violated: If software vulnerabilities exist or privileged nodes are compromised, all security properties fail. Non-trusted parties can derive operational keys, access group communications, and impersonate group members. Properties PROP_001, PROP_002, PROP_015, PROP_018, PROP_043 immediately fail.

assumptions.statement: Crypto_KDF and Crypto_DRBG are cryptographically secure and produce unpredictable outputs with proper entropy.
assumptions.impact_if_violated: If KDF is weak or predictable, attackers can derive operational keys from known inputs without possessing epoch keys. If DRBG lacks entropy, epoch keys are guessable. Properties PROP_004, PROP_016, PROP_017, PROP_028 fail. All key derivation security breaks.

assumptions.statement: Administrator has reliable network connectivity to all group members and sufficient centralized knowledge to coordinate group membership without collisions.
assumptions.impact_if_violated: If administrator cannot reach nodes during key rotation, those nodes become orphaned and lose group connectivity after current keys expire. If multiple administrators operate without coordination, Group ID collisions occur. Properties PROP_003, PROP_023, PROP_035, PROP_041 fail.

assumptions.statement: Network propagation time is bounded and key propagation interval is set sufficiently large for all nodes to receive updates before key activation.
assumptions.impact_if_violated: If propagation interval is too short or network delays are excessive, nodes do not receive new keys before activation. Group communication partially fails during rotation as some nodes use old keys while others use new keys. Properties PROP_023, PROP_031 fail.

assumptions.statement: Nodes correctly implement the state machine for key rotation and maintain consistent state during updates and time progression.
assumptions.impact_if_violated: If state machine implementation is incorrect, nodes may skip states, use wrong keys as current, or fail to recognize epoch activations. Communication failures occur even with correct key distribution. Properties PROP_020, PROP_032, PROP_036 fail.

assumptions.statement: ACL mechanisms correctly enforce access restrictions on key material attributes in Group Key Management Cluster.
assumptions.impact_if_violated: If ACLs are misconfigured or bypassable, any node can read epoch keys and self-admit to groups without authorization. Administrator loses control over group membership. Properties PROP_001, PROP_029, PROP_034 fail completely.

assumptions.statement: Time synchronization among time-capable nodes is sufficiently accurate that clock drift does not cause significant skew during key propagation intervals.
assumptions.impact_if_violated: If clock drift is significant, time-synced nodes activate new keys at different real-world times despite synchronized start times. Early activators cannot communicate with late activators during transition window. Properties PROP_022, PROP_023 partially fail.

assumptions.statement: Nodes without time synchronization contact Administrator frequently enough (at least as fast as key propagation interval) to receive epoch key updates.
assumptions.impact_if_violated: If unsynchronized nodes (e.g., sleepy ICDs) do not contact Administrator within propagation interval, they miss key rotation and lose group connectivity. They continue using old keys that other nodes have expired. Properties PROP_030, PROP_031 fail for these nodes.

vulnerabilities_if_violated.vulnerability: Unauthorized epoch key access via privilege escalation or ACL bypass
vulnerabilities_if_violated.attack_vector: Attacker compromises low-privilege node, exploits ACL misconfiguration to read epoch keys from Group Key Management Cluster, derives operational keys for all groups

vulnerabilities_if_violated.vulnerability: Cryptographic attack derives operational keys without epoch key possession
vulnerabilities_if_violated.attack_vector: Attacker exploits weak KDF implementation or analyzes side channels during key derivation to extract epoch keys from operational keys, or brute-forces weak DRBG

vulnerabilities_if_violated.vulnerability: Group ID collision causes cross-group message leakage
vulnerabilities_if_violated.attack_vector: Two administrators independently allocate same Group ID. Node joins both groups. Messages intended for Group A are processed by Group B with potentially disastrous consequences (e.g., unlock command meant for test environment affects production locks)

vulnerabilities_if_violated.vulnerability: Predictable epoch keys enable precomputation attack
vulnerabilities_if_violated.attack_vector: Weak DRBG or insufficient entropy causes predictable epoch keys. Attacker precomputes rainbow table of operational keys, instantly derives future keys from observed traffic patterns

vulnerabilities_if_violated.vulnerability: Sender uses future epoch key, receivers cannot decrypt
vulnerabilities_if_violated.attack_vector: Attacker with compromised admin credentials pushes future-dated key and triggers premature activation on subset of nodes. Targeted nodes cannot communicate with rest of group, causing denial of service

vulnerabilities_if_violated.vulnerability: Non-atomic key update leaves old keys accessible, revocation fails
vulnerabilities_if_violated.attack_vector: Race condition during key update allows attacker to interrupt atomic operation. Old keys remain valid. Administrator believes member is revoked but member retains indefinite access via old key

vulnerabilities_if_violated.vulnerability: IPK removal permanently exposes node identities
vulnerabilities_if_violated.attack_vector: Attacker with admin access removes IPK key set. All node identities become traceable across all communications. Privacy model collapses, enabling device tracking and behavioral analysis

vulnerabilities_if_violated.vulnerability: Cross-fabric key reuse enables multi-fabric compromise
vulnerabilities_if_violated.attack_vector: Implementation shortcuts share epoch keys across fabrics. Attacker compromises one fabric, immediately gains access to all operational groups in all fabrics sharing keys. Multi-tenancy isolation breaks

vulnerabilities_if_violated.vulnerability: Revoked member retains group access indefinitely
vulnerabilities_if_violated.attack_vector: Administrator stops distributing new keys to revoked node but never explicitly deletes old keys from node. Node continues using old keys. Receivers still accept those keys per PROP_024. Revocation never takes effect

vulnerabilities_if_violated.vulnerability: Premature key activation causes group partition
vulnerabilities_if_violated.attack_vector: Network delay or administrator error causes new key activation before all nodes receive it. Group splits into two partitions: those with new key and those without. No cross-partition communication possible until next rotation

vulnerabilities_if_violated.vulnerability: Denial of service via Group Session ID collision
vulnerabilities_if_violated.attack_vector: Attacker analyzes Group Session ID derivation, crafts operational key with same 16-bit hash as legitimate key. Implementation uses Group Session ID alone to locate key, selects wrong key, decrypt fails. Legitimate messages rejected
Implementation skips MIC verification for performance. Attacker sends forged groupcast commands without any key knowledge. All commands executed as if from legitimate group member

vulnerabilities_if_violated.vulnerability: Rogue administrator self-elevation via key generation
vulnerabilities_if_violated.attack_vector: Node generates its own epoch keys, adds them to local key set, and creates parallel shadow group. Other nodes with misconfigured ACLs pull keys from rogue administrator. Unauthorized parallel group operates with same Group ID

vulnerabilities_if_violated.vulnerability: State machine desynchronization causes mutual decrypt failure
vulnerabilities_if_violated.attack_vector: Implementation bug causes state machine to skip EpochActivate state. Some nodes treat key as Current while others treat as New. Nodes encrypt with different keys, all inter-node communication fails

vulnerabilities_if_violated.vulnerability: Epoch key exfiltration via attribute read
vulnerabilities_if_violated.attack_vector: Attacker exploits ACL vulnerability to read Group Key Management Cluster attributes. All epoch keys and group configurations exfiltrated. Attacker joins any group, decrypts all historical traffic if captured

fsmReferenceProperties.critical_property: PROP_032 - If state machine does not correctly transition to AdminRefresh, key set initialization is incomplete and nodes cannot establish initial group communication
PROP_032 - If EpochActivate transition fails or occurs prematurely, senders and receivers use different epoch keys causing group partition
PROP_009 - If AdminUpdate does not atomically remove old keys, revocation mechanism fails and expelled members retain access
PROP_005 - Senders must use current key from KeyActive state; using wrong key makes messages undecryptable
PROP_024 - Keys remain in KeyInstalled state until explicit deletion; premature removal breaks ongoing communications
PROP_018 - Membership state determined solely by epoch key possession; any other mechanism creates authorization bypass
PROP_036 - Commissioning sequence must complete in order; skipping steps leaves node in inconsistent state unable to send or receive

additionalInsights: The specification exhibits several subtle vulnerability patterns: (1) Temporal Asymmetry Risk - senders use 'current' key but receivers accept 'any installed' key creates window where revocation is ineffective until explicit deletion; this is documented but creates operational burden. (2) Time-Sync Assumption Weakness - specification provides workaround for unsynchronized nodes but assumes they contact administrator within propagation interval; network partitions or sleeping devices violate this, yet no fallback mechanism exists. (3) Atomic Operation Without Transaction Guarantees - specification mandates atomic key replacement but provides no mechanism (e.g., two-phase commit) to ensure atomicity in practice; relies entirely on implementation correctness. (4) Revocation Ambiguity - revocation can occur via (a) withholding new keys OR (b) explicit deletion, but no guidance on when to use which; administrators may believe revocation is effective when it is not. (5) Group Session ID is security-critical optimization - the 2^-16 collision probability seems acceptable but creates exploitable DoS surface if implementation uses it as sole locator despite specification forbidding this. (6) IPK irremovability creates operational risk - if IPK is compromised, there is no recovery path short of decommissioning entire fabric. (7) The state machine diagram shows clean transitions but does not address error states (what if EpochActivate never occurs due to clock failure?). (8) Multi-administrator coordination is assumed but not enforced - the specification states administrator 'has sufficient access to centralized knowledge' without defining mechanism; this assumption fails in federated or multi-admin scenarios.
